title: _multiprocessing: concurrent SemLock create/destroy flags a data race on glibc's __sem_mappings tree (TSan false positive)
description: Creating a _multiprocessing.SemLock calls glibc sem_open() -> tsearch (insert into the process-global __sem_mappings tree); destroying it calls sem_close() -> tdelete (remove). Concurrent create/destroy makes TSan report a race on that tree. glibc actually serializes all tree access with __sem_mappings_lock (an lll/futex lock TSan does not intercept), so this is a false positive -- not a CPython bug and not a SemLock use-after-free.
sites: Modules/_multiprocessing/semaphore.c _multiprocessing_SemLock_impl:516 (SEM_CREATE = sem_open() -> glibc tsearch(): malloc + insert a __sem_mappings node)
Objects/object.c _Py_Dealloc:3319 (calls tp_dealloc = semlock_dealloc -> SEM_CLOSE = sem_close() -> glibc tdelete(): free + remove a __sem_mappings node)
race_kind: false-positive (uninstrumented glibc internal low-level lock: __sem_mappings_lock / lll_lock is invisible to TSan)
shared_state: glibc process-global __sem_mappings red-black tree (named-POSIX-semaphore mapping table); NOT the SemLock PyObject
severity: none (TSan false positive; no real data race, no memory unsafety, runs clean on non-TSan builds)
repro_reliability: deterministic (exit 66; 8 threads x 4000 create/destroy of _multiprocessing.SemLock with unlink=True)
suggested_fix: None in CPython. Suppress via TSan suppressions file (race:tsearch / race:tdelete / race:sem_open / race:sem_close, or called_from_lib:libc.so), or use a TSan-instrumented libc. A CPython-side lock around sem_open/sem_close would only mask harmless noise.
notes: Root-caused from C source + glibc disassembly: sem_open/sem_close bracket every tsearch/tdelete/twalk on __sem_mappings with acquire/release on the same lock word (0x2187d8 = __sem_mappings_lock), including __lll_lock_wait_private/__lll_lock_wake_private slow paths. TSan interposes pthread_mutex/rwlock but not glibc's lll_lock, hence the phantom race. Same class covers all named-POSIX-semaphore churn: multiprocessing Lock/RLock/Semaphore/BoundedSemaphore/Condition/Event all build on _multiprocessing.SemLock. The auto-seeded 'SemLock use-after-free' framing is incorrect: the racing address is a libc tree node, and the two SemLock PyObjects are distinct (one created, another destroyed).
assessment: NOT a CPython bug — glibc __sem_mappings tree race under glibc's invisible __sem_mappings_lock (same class as tzset). Suppressed in catalog/suppressions.txt.

related_issues.note: merged: critical sections on SemLock __enter__/__exit__ only — unrelated to this create/destroy libc-tree FP
